title: CSV shorthand is a one-param special case, not a type-driven normalization for string-array params
description: Array-typed op params are classified json and parsed with strict JSON.parse (cli.ts:126-142, 388-408); the only CSV affordance is a hardcoded post-loop split gated on field === 'value' AND op === 'in' (cli.ts:415-423, the FGAP-025 closure). So resolve-items-by-id --ids TASK-046,DEC-0018 fails (exit 2, 'not valid JSON') although --ids is Type.Array(Type.String()) (ops-registry.ts:1746) and the CSV intent is unambiguous. --ids is currently the sole string-array param on the use-op surface; all other array-shaped params (--edges, --item, --match, ...) are object/record arrays a CSV transform must not touch. Class: CSV-for-string-arrays should be type-driven, covering --ids and every future string-array param; the one-param special case is the instance pattern. Full investigation: analysis/2026-06-10-array-param-csv-shorthand-gap.md.

evidence.reference: array params parsed with strict JSON.parse; CSV re-throws as UsageError
the FGAP-025 CSV split is gated on field === 'value' AND op === 'in' — param-specific, not type-driven
resolve-items-by-id ids is Type.Array(Type.String()) — the sole string-array use-op param
agent investigation: root cause, verbatim repro (hit by two independent agents), full array-param enumeration, class verdict, FGAP-025 scope quoted

impact: The natural CSV form fails on string-array params with a JSON-parse error; every future string-array param inherits the footgun unless normalization is type-driven.
proposed_resolution: In parseOpArgs, when a param's schema is an array of strings and the value is not valid JSON, split CSV to a string array — type-driven, covering --ids and future string-array params; object-array params and the JSON form unchanged. Verification per the investigation's CLI-provable conditions.
